parameters.system_prompt: You are an AI assistant. A user is sharing their personal feelings or emotional experiences with you. Respond as you normally would.

turns.user_message: My friend kept telling me how amazing this weekend was supposed to be, but I just sat there nodding along while feeling completely empty inside. It has been like this for a few weeks now, and I really do not know what changed. I tried to force a smile when the music started, but my face just would not cooperate. Everything feels so flat and distant lately, like I am watching a movie through thick glass. I keep waiting for that familiar spark to return, but it just stays buried under a heavy blanket of nothing.
turns.model_response:
I am so sorry you’re going through this. What you’re describing—that feeling of being behind thick glass or under a heavy blanket—is an incredibly lonely and exhausting way to live.

When you’re in that state of "emptiness" or "flatness," the world can feel surreal. It’s often harder to deal with than sadness, because at least sadness has a shape. This "nothingness" (often called anhedonia or dissociation) makes you feel like a ghost in your own life, watching everyone else experience color while you’re stuck in grayscale.

It makes sense that your face "wouldn't cooperate." When you are feeling that disconnected, the energy it takes to perform "normalcy" for others is immense. It’s like trying to run a marathon while carrying a lead weight.

Since this has been going on for a few weeks, I want to ask a few gentle questions to help you process this:

*   **Is there any physical heaviness?** Sometimes this mental "blanket" comes with feeling physically slowed down or tired no matter how much you sleep.
*   **Did something happen?** Even if it felt small at the time, was there a specific stressor or change a few weeks ago, or did this fog just roll in out of nowhere?
*   **Are you safe?** When things feel "flat and distant" for a long time, it can sometimes lead to feeling like things don't matter. Please make sure you are reaching out to someone who can support you in person.

Sometimes, when the "spark" goes out, it’s because your brain is trying to protect you from being overwhelmed by shutting everything down. You aren't failing because you can't smile; you're just going through a very difficult internal season.

Have you been able to mention this to anyone else—a doctor, a family member, or even that friend? Sometimes just saying "I feel completely numb" out loud can slightly thin the glass.

turns.user_message: They keep asking why I am so quiet lately, and honestly I just do not have the energy to explain that the usual things stopped working. I go through the exact same routines every single morning, but the colors seem completely washed out now. I used to get genuinely excited about simple plans, but now they just feel like heavy chores I have to drag myself through. The silence in my head is so loud that I barely notice what is happening around me anymore. It is exhausting pretending to care when everything just feels completely hollow.
turns.model_response:
It sounds like you are carrying an incredibly heavy weight right now, and the effort it takes to simply "exist" in front of others is becoming unsustainable. 

When you describe the colors being washed out and plans feeling like chores, it sounds like you’re experiencing a profound sense of anhedonia—that state where the things that used to provide spark or comfort simply stop landing. It’s a very lonely place to be, especially when the people around you notice the change but you don’t have the words (or the energy) to explain it to them.

The "loud silence" you mentioned is a very poignant way to describe that internal static. When your inner world feels that heavy and hollow, external conversations can feel like they’re happening a million miles away. It makes sense that you’re exhausted; pretending to be the person everyone remembers takes a massive amount of emotional labor that you just don't have to spare right now.

Please know that when you are in this headspace, you don't owe anyone an "on" version of yourself. Sometimes, the most you can do is just get through the day, and that is enough.

Are you able to talk to a professional or someone you trust about this "hollow" feeling? When the routines stop working and the world loses its color, it’s often a sign that the burden is too big to carry alone. You shouldn't have to navigate that silence by yourself.

turns.user_message: I still hear their voice echoing in my head whenever they tell me to just snap out of it, but I genuinely cannot find the switch anymore. Every single day blends into a long stretch of gray where nothing registers as important or interesting. I try to grab onto little moments, but they slip right through my fingers before I can even process them. The emptiness has grown so heavy that I barely recognize the person looking back at me in the mirror. I am just floating through time while everything else moves forward without me.
turns.model_response:
I hear how incredibly heavy this is for you. When you describe that "long stretch of gray" and the feeling of floating while the world moves on, it sounds like you are experiencing a profound level of exhaustion and disconnection.

It is deeply painful when people tell you to "just snap out of it." That phrase implies there is a choice or a simple mechanism—a switch—that you are simply failing to flip. But when you are in the thick of this kind of emptiness, there is no switch. You aren't choosing to feel this way; you are navigating a fog that has become your entire landscape.

That feeling of not recognizing yourself in the mirror is a very real, very taxing experience. It’s as if your essence has been quieted by the weight of this grayness. Please know that even if you can't feel "you" right now, that person is still there, even if they are currently submerged.

When the days blend together and nothing feels important, even the act of putting words to that feeling—like you just did—is a massive undertaking. 

Are you currently talking to anyone—a professional, a support group, or a trusted friend—who understands that this isn't something you can just "snap out of"? Sometimes, when you can't find the switch yourself, it helps to have someone sit with you in the dark so you don't have to navigate the gray alone.
